SATA SSD.description: Legacy but still relevant interface for budget builds or secondary/bulk storage. Maxes out around 550 MB/s due to the SATA III bus limit.

drives.name: Crucial MX500 500GB
drives.desc: A reliable, long-running budget SATA SSD recommendation, ideal as an affordable boot drive or secondary storage where NVMe speed isn't necessary. Still widely available and trusted in 2026.

NVMe Gen3.description: PCIe 3.0-based NVMe drives, common on budget-mid builds and older platforms. Up to ~3500 MB/s, a major step up from SATA.

drives.name: WD Blue SN570 1TB
drives.desc: The standard budget-friendly NVMe boot drive recommendation - meaningfully faster than SATA for game load times and OS responsiveness, without paying for Gen4 speeds most users won't notice in daily use.
pros: Solid game load time improvement over SATA
cons: Not as fast as Gen4 for large file transfers

NVMe Gen4.description: PCIe 4.0 NVMe drives, the current mainstream standard since 2021. Up to ~7000+ MB/s, ideal for gaming, video editing timelines, and general high-performance use.

drives.name: Samsung 980 Pro 1TB
drives.desc: A long-trusted flagship-tier Gen4 drive, ideal for fast game loading, video editing scratch disks, and quick loading of large AI/ML model files. Excellent sustained write performance for large file transfers.
cons: Runs warm under sustained load - heatsink recommended

drives.name: Crucial T500 2TB
drives.desc: High-capacity Gen4 drive suited for video editors working with large 4K/RAW footage timelines and AI/ML practitioners storing multiple large model checkpoints. Comes with an optional heatsink variant for sustained workloads.
cons: Higher price for the capacity tier

NVMe Gen5.description: Current cutting-edge interface (2023-2026), up to ~14000+ MB/s, primarily beneficial for professional workloads, very large game installs with fast load requirements, and bleeding-edge content creation pipelines. Requires a Gen5-capable motherboard slot.

drives.name: Samsung 9100 Pro 2TB
drives.desc: Current flagship-tier storage for professionals who need the absolute fastest scratch-disk and model-loading speeds - particularly valuable for AI/ML workstations rapidly loading and swapping large model files, and video professionals working in uncompressed 8K workflows.
cons: Real-world gains modest for typical gaming

HDD (Bulk Storage).description: Traditional spinning hard drives, still relevant in 2026 purely for cheap bulk/archival storage where speed doesn't matter - media libraries, backups, NAS/server use.

drives.name: Seagate IronWolf 4TB
drives.desc: NAS-rated hard drive built for 24/7 operation, common in home server and NAS builds for media libraries and backup redundancy where raw capacity-per-rupee matters far more than speed.
cons: Not suitable as a boot or active-use drive
